title: Testing and adjusting pulp consistency for hand papermaking
subtitle: How to read a slurry and know when it is ready to pull from
excerpt: The right pulp consistency for a given sheet weight is not a fixed number; it depends on the fibre type, the beating level, and the mould size. This tutorial covers three simple on-the-spot tests that tell the papermaker whether the vat is at the right concentration before pulling sheets.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), public domain, Internet Archive. Hunter describes pulp consistency and its relationship to sheet weight throughout his documentation of East Asian and Western papermaking methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: The mass of paper per square metre, expressed in grams (gsm). Ordinary copy paper is 80 gsm; standard cartridge is 90-120 gsm; tissue paper is 15-30 gsm.

content.text: Pulp consistency: the ratio of fibre to water in the vat: directly determines

. Too much fibre produces a heavy, uneven sheet with thick areas where the fibres clustered; too little produces a tissue-thin result when a heavier sheet was intended. Three visual and tactile tests help calibrate the vat without weighing the dry sheet.

Scoop a glass of vat water and hold it to the light. The suspension should be uniformly milky: evenly cloudy throughout, with no visible fibre clumps and no clear water at the top. If clear water floats on top, the pulp is sitting rather than dispersed; stir again. If the suspension is opaque rather than translucent milky, the concentration is probably too high for standard-weight sheets: dilute with a litre of water and retest. For tissue-weight work, the glass should barely show any cloudiness.

Dip the mould and deckle as for a normal sheet, drain, and remove the deckle. Tilt the mould slightly and look at the wet fibre mat from the side. A standard sheet: targeting 80-100 gsm dry: should show a continuous white mat covering the mesh with no gaps or thin spots. If you can see the mesh through the mat in places, the vat is too dilute; add more blended pulp and stir. If the mat looks thick and lumpy, dilute with water.

Count seconds from the moment the mould clears the vat surface until water stops actively draining from the mesh. For standard cotton-linter sheets, drain time should be 5-8 seconds. Under 5 seconds: the pulp is over-dilute or over-beaten, producing a thin sheet. Over 12 seconds: the pulp is under-dilute or the fibre is very long (hemp, kozo), and the sheet may be thick. Adjust the vat accordingly and test again before pulling finished sheets.

The vat concentration drops with each sheet pulled because the sheet removes fibre from the water. For a consistent batch, add a small amount of blended pulp back to the vat every third or fourth sheet to maintain concentration. The glass test is the fastest check: scoop and look before each pull once you have established the right appearance for the target weight.
